title: Introduction and Background
text:
Automated literature review represents an emerging research area at the intersection of natural language processing, information retrieval, and scholarly knowledge synthesis. The fundamental challenge addressed by this work is the increasing volume of scientific publications that overwhelms manual review processes, prompting researchers to explore computational approaches for tasks including citation screening, evidence extraction, and knowledge synthesis.

This comparative survey examines three representative systems: an NLP plus LLM retrieval approach (p1), an LLM-based RAG tool (p2), and an NLP classification system for citation screening (p3). These systems collectively represent the methodological spectrum from traditional NLP pipelines to retrieval-augmented generation architectures. The scope encompasses automated literature review tasks, knowledge synthesis, and citation screening across academic corpora including custom collections, arXiv, and PubMed.

The motivation for systematic comparison stems from three critical gaps in current literature. First, English-language dominance in evaluation (g1) limits understanding of cross-lingual generalization. Second, metric reporting heterogeneity (g2) undermines reliable cross-study comparison—ROUGE-L, F1, precision, and AUC are reported with inconsistent calculation methods. Third, limited evidence grounding (g3) means systems claim performance without verifiable evidence chains, raising questions about reproducibility and reliability. This review synthesizes methodological approaches, evaluation practices, and grounding mechanisms to articulate consolidated research gaps and future directions.

title: Methodological Approaches
text:
The methodological landscape of automated literature review systems spans three distinct architectural families: RAG-based approaches, NLP pipelines, and hybrid combinations thereof. This section evaluates their architectural differences and performance implications.

Paper p1 employs a hybrid RAG plus NLP pipeline approach, combining NLP extraction with LLM retrieval for knowledge synthesis tasks on custom corpora. The claim that combining NLP extraction with LLM retrieval achieves higher coverage than keyword baselines (c1) reflects the complementary strengths of rule-based extraction and neural retrieval. However, the system is constrained by dependency on LLM quality (p2 notes this limitation) and English-only evaluation.

Paper p2 presents a pure RAG architecture focused on information extraction from arXiv. The retrieval augmentation approach explicitly aims to ground claims in source evidence (c2), representing a methodological commitment to evidence traceability. This architectural choice distinguishes RAG systems from extractive NLP pipelines by incorporating citation context and document-level relationships.

Paper p3 demonstrates a traditional NLP classification pipeline for citation screening on PubMed data. The claim that traditional NLP achieves competitive F1 but lags LLM on recall (c3) highlights a fundamental trade-off: pipeline approaches offer interpretability and computational efficiency but may sacrifice the contextual understanding that LLM-based methods provide.

The divergence among these approaches reveals that architectural choice involves explicit trade-offs between retrieval precision, computational cost, and evidence grounding capability. Hybrid architectures attempt to capture benefits of both paradigms but introduce additional complexity in integration.

title: Evaluation Metrics and Reporting Practices
text:
Metric heterogeneity across automated literature review systems presents a significant barrier to cross-study comparison. This section analyzes how ROUGE-L, F1, precision, and AUC are employed and identifies inconsistencies that undermine reliable benchmarking.

Paper p1 reports performance using ROUGE-L and F1 metrics on custom corpora. ROUGE-L measures longest common subsequence overlap between generated and reference summaries, while F1 balances precision and recall in classification or extraction tasks. However, the calculation specifications—whether ROUGE-L uses sentence-level or document-level references, or whether F1 operates at token or entity level—are not documented, limiting interpretability.

Paper p2 employs F1 and precision for evaluation on arXiv data. The precision-focused reporting aligns with the system's emphasis on evidence grounding—prioritizing accurate claim-attribution over comprehensive coverage. Yet the discrepancy between p2's precision-oriented approach and p1's balanced F1 reporting reflects unexamined variation in what constitutes satisfactory performance.

Paper p3 uses F1 and AUC for citation screening on PubMed, introducing AUC (Area Under the Curve) as a threshold-independent measure of ranking capability. This choice captures retrieval performance beyond binary classification but further compounds comparison challenges.

The consequence of metric heterogeneity (g2) is that cross-study comparison becomes unreliable—a system reporting 0.85 ROUGE-L cannot be meaningfully compared to one reporting 0.85 AUC without understanding whether both measure the same construct. The field lacks standardized evaluation protocols, preventing systematic progress tracking.

title: Language Coverage and Cross-lingual Generalization
text:
English-language dominance in evaluation represents a critical limitation of current automated literature review systems. This section assesses the extent of this dominance and reviews evidence for multilingual capability claims.

Paper p1 explicitly lists English-only as a limitation, indicating that the system was developed and evaluated exclusively on English-language corpora. This constraint is particularly significant given that scientific literature is produced globally, with substantial contributions in Chinese, German, Japanese, Spanish, and other languages. The claim of higher coverage than keyword baselines (c1) applies only within English-language boundaries.

Paper p2 and p3 do not report multilingual evaluation, suggesting their claims are likewise English-centric. The arXiv and PubMed datasets used for evaluation are predominantly English-language sources, meaning performance claims cannot generalize to non-English scientific literature without explicit verification.

The consequence of English-language dominance (g1) is that the field lacks verified cross-lingual generalization. Systems may perform adequately on English but fail catastrophically on languages with different morphological structures, writing systems, or citation conventions. The verified gap statement that cross-lingual generalization remains unverified is substantiated by the complete absence of multilingual evaluation across the reviewed systems. This limitation prevents automated literature review tools from serving the global research community and from synthesizing evidence across linguistic boundaries.

title: Evidence Grounding and Verification
text:
Evidence grounding refers to the ability of automated literature review systems to connect their outputs to source material through verifiable evidence chains. This section examines how systems achieve this and evaluates the reliability of claimed grounding mechanisms.

Paper p2 makes the explicit methodological claim that retrieval augmentation grounds claims in source evidence (c2). This represents a deliberate architectural choice: by retrieving relevant source passages before generation, RAG systems can theoretically cite evidence for their outputs. However, the limitation that LLM quality is performance-dependent (p2) raises concerns about whether grounding is reliable or merely present.

Paper p1's claim of higher coverage (c1) lacks explicit evidence chain reporting. While the system combines NLP extraction with LLM retrieval, the paper does not demonstrate that coverage improvements are traceable to specific source documents. This distinction matters for reproducibility—without evidence chains, claims of performance cannot be independently verified.

Paper p3's citation screening approach (c3) offers implicit grounding through classification decisions, but the evidence chain is limited to document-level relevance rather than claim-level attribution. The single dataset limitation further constrains generalizability.

The verified gap of limited evidence grounding (g3) is substantiated by inconsistent reporting: only p2 explicitly addresses evidence chaining, while p1 and p3 lack transparent evidence mechanisms. This prevents users from assessing reliability of outputs and undermines trust in automated literature review systems for scientific knowledge synthesis.

title: Synthesis and Open Research Problems
text:
This review reveals that automated literature review remains an emerging field characterized by methodological diversity but constrained by three verified gaps that limit practical utility and scientific progress.

The English-language dominance gap (g1) is substantiated across all reviewed systems: p1 explicitly reports English-only evaluation, while p2 and p3 lack multilingual reporting. This prevents the field from addressing global scientific literature synthesis needs. Cross-lingual generalization remains entirely unverified, representing a high-severity barrier to widespread adoption.

The metric reporting heterogeneity gap (g2) manifests through inconsistent use of ROUGE-L (p1), F1 (p1, p2, p3), precision (p2), and AUC (p3) without standardized calculation methods. The consequence is unreliable cross-study comparison—the field cannot determine which architectural approach genuinely outperforms others. This medium-severity gap requires consensus on evaluation protocols.

The evidence grounding gap (g3) is evidenced by p2's explicit but potentially unreliable grounding mechanism alongside p1 and p3's absence of evidence chain reporting. Systems claim performance without verifiable evidence chains, undermining reproducibility and user trust.

Future research should prioritize: developing multilingual evaluation benchmarks to address g1; establishing standardized metric calculation and reporting protocols to address g2; and implementing transparent evidence verification mechanisms to address g3. The hybrid architectural approaches showing promise (p1) warrant investigation across diverse languages and datasets, with rigorous evidence grounding requirements. Without addressing these consolidated gaps, automated literature review systems will remain limited in reliability and generalizability.
